tossups.question: A treatise named for this place preserves an argument based on the “indiscriminability” of pairs of eggs, as well as the “inactivity objection” that faced a position named for this place. A leader of this place opposed ideas of “natural justice” since a shipwrecked sailor would be a fool to not kill a weaker man clinging to a plank of wood. Augustine’s first work is a dialogue “against” a tradition named for this place that promoted reliance on pithanon, or the “probable,” in the face of acatalepsia. Due to scholarchs like Arcesilaus (“ar-sess-ih-LAY-us”) and Carneades, this place names the more extreme of the two main traditions of ancient skepticism. Justinian closed this place, to which a plucked chicken was once brought to ridicule a definition of man as a “featherless biped.” For 10 points, name this school in a grove outside Athens that was founded by Plato.
tossups.question_sanitized: A treatise named for this place preserves an argument based on the "indiscriminability" of pairs of eggs, as well as the "inactivity objection" that faced a position named for this place. A leader of this place opposed ideas of "natural justice" since a shipwrecked sailor would be a fool to not kill a weaker man clinging to a plank of wood. Augustine's first work is a dialogue "against" a tradition named for this place that promoted reliance on pithanon, or the "probable," in the face of acatalepsia. Due to scholarchs like Arcesilaus ("ar-sess-ih-LAY-us") and Carneades, this place names the more extreme of the two main traditions of ancient skepticism. Justinian closed this place, to which a plucked chicken was once brought to ridicule a definition of man as a "featherless biped." For 10 points, name this school in a grove outside Athens that was founded by Plato.
tossups.answer: Academy [or Platonic Academy; or Akademía; accept New Academy or Third Academy or Old Academy; accept academic skepticism or Against the Academicians or Academica; prompt on the School of Athens until “Athens” is read] (The first sentence refers to Cicero’s Academica.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Academy [or Platonic Academy; or Akademia; accept New Academy or Third Academy or Old Academy; accept academic skepticism or Against the Academicians or Academica; prompt on the School of Athens until "Athens" is read] (The first sentence refers to Cicero's Academica.)

tossups.question: Mazzola created a 3D CGI reconstruction of this painting in order to propose that people within it are standing in a certain shape, which, according to Fichtner, encodes a secret theorem. That shape, a six-pointed star, is drawn by a man who likely helped the artist with most of the architecture in this painting. A tondo above this painting reads causarum cognitio, and it is grouped with three others that depict the (*) arts, theology, and law. A self-portrait of the artist hidden in this work places him behind some men with globes. One of the central figures in this painting, which is placed across from La Disputa, extends his hand towards the viewer, while the other points up. For 10 points, name this fresco in the Stanza della Segnatura that features many philosophers and intellectuals including Plato and Aristotle, a work by Raphael.
tossups.question_sanitized: Mazzola created a 3D CGI reconstruction of this painting in order to propose that people within it are standing in a certain shape, which, according to Fichtner, encodes a secret theorem. That shape, a six-pointed star, is drawn by a man who likely helped the artist with most of the architecture in this painting. A tondo above this painting reads causarum cognitio, and it is grouped with three others that depict the (*) arts, theology, and law. A self-portrait of the artist hidden in this work places him behind some men with globes. One of the central figures in this painting, which is placed across from La Disputa, extends his hand towards the viewer, while the other points up. For 10 points, name this fresco in the Stanza della Segnatura that features many philosophers and intellectuals including Plato and Aristotle, a work by Raphael.

bonuses.leadin: The story from Vasari’s Lives of the Artist in which Giotto draws a perfect circle for the pope was likely inspired by Pliny the Elder’s anecdote in which this painter impressed Protogenes with a single fine line. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The story from Vasari's Lives of the Artist in which Giotto draws a perfect circle for the pope was likely inspired by Pliny the Elder's anecdote in which this painter impressed Protogenes with a single fine line. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Ancient Greek painter of Aphrodite Anadyomene (“an-uh-dy-OM-uh-nee”). Botticelli painted a “Calumny” modeled on a lost work by this painter from Kos.

A challenge posed by this author led many Renaissance artists to create versions of The Calumny of Apelles. This author devised geometric ideas on perspective in De pictura and built on Vitruvius in On the Art of Building.
Apelles may have been the model for Raphael’s depiction of himself in this painting, in which he stands next to a man modeled on Protogenes. Plato points toward the sky in this painting.

Leon Battista Alberti
The School of Athens [or Scuola di Atene]

bonuses.leadin: Joseph Kosuth (“koh-SOOT”) asserted that “art only exists conceptually” in a tract titled Art After [this field]. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Joseph Kosuth ("koh-SOOT") asserted that "art only exists conceptually" in a tract titled Art After [this field]. For 10 points each:
parts: The conceptual artist Adrian Piper has also worked as a tenured professor in what field? In a 16th-century painting, two practitioners of this field respectively gesture at the sky and the ground.

Piper’s art on the experience of passing includes a piece in which she gave these objects to people who made racist remarks. Though it later became a stamp design, Robert Indiana first put his LOVE image on these objects.
Art After Philosophy traces the birth of conceptual art to the “readymades” of this artist, who signed a urinal “R. Mutt” and called it Fountain.

bonuses.leadin: This figure’s red cloak is sometimes interpreted as symbolizing fire, in contrast to the bright blue cloak of the man to his right. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this figure who is modeled on Leonardo da Vinci in a Renaissance painting in which he holds a book in his left hand and points into the air with his right.
That depiction of Plato is from this painting, whose artist, Raphael, flattered himself by giving his own features to its depiction of the master artist Apelles.

Vasari suggests that this artist was the model for Euclid in The School of Athens. This artist designed the Palazzo Caprini (“pah-LOTS-oh ka-PREE-nee”) where Raphael once lived, as well as a small, circular chapel commissioned by Ferdinand and Isabella of Spain.
answers: Donato Bramante [or Bramante Lazzari] (The latter building is the Tempietto.)
answers_sanitized: Plato [or Platon]
The School of Athens [or La scuola d'Atene]
Donato Bramante [or Bramante Lazzari] (The latter building is the Tempietto.)

bonuses.leadin: Anton Raphael Mengs created a copy of this painting for the Duke of Northumberland, though Mengs titled his copy Philosophy. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Raphael fresco in which Leonardo da Vinci appears as Plato pointing a finger into the air.

Mengs tricked this scholar into believing his fresco Jupiter and Ganymede was from ancient Rome to mock his theories. This German "father of archaeology"'s The History of Art in Antiquity was one of the first examinations of ancient art.

A Mengs painting depicts this woman staring at a cross carved out of a branch. This woman, who sits with a skull in her lap and stares at a candle flickering in a mirror in a Georges de la Tour painting, appears emaciated in a 1453 sculpture made of white poplar.
answers_sanitized: The School of Athens
Johann Joachim Winckelmann
Mary Magdalene [or The Penitent Magdalene; prompt on Mary]

bonuses.leadin: Many Northern Renaissance artists, including Baldung and Cranach the Elder, painted depictions of a myth in which a whip-brandishing woman named Phyllis rides this man like a horse. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this man. Julius Held cited other depictions of this man in expensive jewelry to argue that a Rembrandt painting of a man with a gold chain depicts him rather than, as Simon Schama claimed, Apelles.

Depictions of Phyllis riding Aristotle were examples of this topos popular in Northern Renaissance art. This motif is also exemplified by paintings of Virgil in his basket and Judith with the head of Holofernes.
Aristotle, gesturing toward the ground, converses with his teacher, Plato, who points up at the sky, in this Raphael fresco depicting many ancient Greek mathematicians and philosophers.
answers: Aristotle [or Aristoteles] (The Dutch painting is Rembrandt’s Aristotle Contemplating a Bust of Homer.)
the “Power of Women” [or the Weibermacht; accept the battle for the trousers or the follies of love; prompt on descriptive answers like strong women or women overcoming men]

bonuses.leadin: While in Athens, this thinker produced the first Latin translations of Pseudo-Dionysius. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this 9th-century Irish philosopher who defined god as universitas rerum, or the totality of things, in his magnum opus Periphyseon, or On the Division of Things.
Eriugena was heavily inspired by this philosophical tradition, which began with Plotinus and held that reality was the emanation of a single principle called “the One.”

Eriugena’s translations helped revive Neoplatonic thought several hundred years after this historical event, which scholars typically use to mark the end of Neoplatonism. Following this event, Damascius and his followers fled to the court of Khosrau.
answers: John Scotus <u>Eriugena</u> [or Joannes <u>Scottigena</u>; prompt on <u>Scot</u>us; do not accept or prompt on “(John) Duns Scotus”]

Justinian I <u>closing</u> the Platonic <u>Academy</u> [accept the <u>School</u> of <u>Athens</u> in place of “the Platonic Academy”; accept any answer mentioning the <u>Academy</u> or <u>School of Athens</u> and the idea of <u>shutting it down</u>]
answers_sanitized: John Scotus Eriugena [or Joannes Scottigena; prompt on Scotus; do not accept or prompt on "(John) Duns Scotus"]
Neoplatonism [prompt on "platonism"]

Justinian I closing the Platonic Academy [accept the School of Athens in place of "the Platonic Academy"; accept any answer mentioning the Academy or School of Athens and the idea of shutting it down]

bonuses.leadin: One of these works done on fruitwood is named for the flowers held up by a baby boy in the center, and not on the hue in the mother’s cheeks. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these paintings by a specific artist, one “of the Pinks.” That artist also painted versions of those paintings “of the Meadow” and ones named for Sistine and Ansidei.
Raphael may be best known for this monumental fresco located in the Vatican’s Stanza della Segnatura [sen-yah-TOO-rah] that shows Plato and Aristotle at the center of a gathering of intellectuals.

Raphael also painted a stern-looking portrait of Elisabetta, a patron of his from this Mantuan royal family. Andrea Mantegna [mon-TANE-yah] painted a group of red-hatted members of this family holding court under its Ludovico II.
answers_sanitized: Madonnas by Raphael
School of Athens [accept Scuola di Atene]
House of Gonzaga

bonuses.leadin: Name some Raphael paintings, for 10 points each.
parts: This painting sits across from La Disputa in the Stanza della Segnatura. It shows a bunch of Greek philosophers lounging around.

The Stanza della Segnatura also houses this painting, in which Apollo plays a violin and is surrounded by the Muses, nine ancient poets, and nine contemporary poets. It shares its name with a fresco by Anton Raphael Mengs.
This Perugino-esque painting has the sun and moon flanking the letters I.N.R.I. St. Jerome kneels at the bottom of this painting, which also shows two angels collecting Jesus’s blood in chalices.
answers: Mond Crucifixion [or:Crocifissione Gavari, Gavari Crucifixion, Crucifixion with the Virgin, Saints and Angels]

The Parnassus [or:IlParnaso]
Mond Crucifixion [or:Crocifissione Gavari, Gavari Crucifixion, Crucifixion with the Virgin, Saints and Angels]
